figures.gloss: The supreme, omnipotent creator of the universe. He is the ultimate source of all power and life force, but is vastly removed from the daily affairs of humanity, having delegated the administration of the world to the mpungos and the ancestral dead.
figures.facet: Unlike Christian omnipotence that demands personal worship, Nzambi is pure, inaccessible cosmological cause. One does not petition him directly for earthly matters, as his sheer magnitude would incinerate petty human concerns.
figures.provenance: Todd Ramón Ochoa, 'Society of the Dead' (2010).

figures.gloss: The mpungo of crossroads, thresholds, and balance. He opens and closes all paths, standing at the gate between the living and the dead. Without his permission, no magic or ritual can cross into the spiritual realm or take effect.
figures.facet: He embodies the unpredictable, kinetic energy of the cosmos. He is not an evil trickster but the prime mover of fate, requiring constant appeasement so his chaotic potential opens roads rather than blocking them.

figures.gloss: The fierce mpungo of iron, war, blood, and technology. He is a tireless worker who cuts through obstacles in both the physical and spiritual jungles. His essence resides in the iron tools, machetes, and railroad spikes housed inside the cauldron.
figures.facet: He is raw, unstoppable kinetic force that demands blood sacrifice to remain heated and active. He does not judge the morality of a request; he merely executes it with the brutal efficiency of a swinging machete.

figures.gloss: The mpungo of lightning, fire, justice, and passionate love. He is a volatile sorcerer-king who strikes from the sky. He is deeply associated with the ceiba tree, which acts as his lightning rod, physical throne, and conduit on earth.
figures.facet: He represents swift, explosive justice and immense magical prowess. Unlike purely celestial storm gods, Siete Rayos is deeply rooted in terrestrial witchcraft, using fire to both illuminate pathways and incinerate enemies.
figures.provenance: Todd Ramón Ochoa, 'Society of the Dead' (2010).

figures.gloss: The mpungo ruling over the vastness of the ocean and all saltwater. She is a maternal, nurturing force who possesses immense, overwhelming power, capable of swallowing ships and washing away the fiercest curses from a practitioner.
figures.facet: She holds the paradox of the ocean: deeply nurturing to her children but ruthlessly destructive when provoked. Her waters are the primordial source of life and the ultimate repository for heavy spiritual cleansing.

figures.gloss: The mpungo who presides over the gates of the cemetery, whirlwinds, and sudden tempests. She is the fierce sovereign of the dead, managing the passage of souls and unleashing chaotic winds when angered or invoked for war.
figures.facet: She rules the physical space where bones reside, the vital currency of Palo. She is an absolute, terrifying matriarch who commands dark 'ndoki' spirits, mediating the hardest, sharpest line between life and death.

figures.gloss: An iron cauldron or clay pot that serves as a self-contained, living microcosm of the universe. Packed with sticks, earths, iron, and the bones of the dead, it is the supreme spiritual engine of Palo sorcery.
figures.facet: The Nganga is not merely an altar; it is a fabricated deity and a localized cosmos. The practitioner feeds it blood to drive its spiritual machinery, forcing reality to bend to the sorcerer's will.
figures.provenance: Todd Ramón Ochoa, 'Society of the Dead' (2010).

figures.gloss: The human spirit—specifically bound to the physical bones housed within the Nganga—with whom the palero makes a magical pact. The nfumbi is the active, restless agent that executes the magic, bridging human intent and cosmic forces.
figures.facet: The nfumbi is not revered in passive ancestor worship; they are an indentured worker or contracted partner. The relationship is strictly transactional, demanding payment in blood and rum for occult labor performed.
figures.provenance: Todd Ramón Ochoa, 'Society of the Dead' (2010).

figures.gloss: Complex chalk or ash diagrams drawn on the ground to invoke specific mpungos or direct spiritual energies. They serve as cosmological maps that chart the four winds, the sun's path, and the boundary between the living and the dead.
figures.facet: A firma does not just symbolize intent; it mechanically directs it. It acts as an active circuitry of spiritual electricity that commands the nfumbi and roots the ephemeral spirit into the exact physical location of the rite.
figures.provenance: Bárbaro Martínez-Ruiz, 'Kongo Graphic Writing and Other Narratives of the Sign' (2013).

figures.gloss: 'Earth-Trembler', the white-clad mpungo of the mountain, purity, and calm judgment, commonly equated with Obatalá across the Cuban reglas.
figures.facet: The cool counterweight the roster otherwise lacks: where Zarabanda and Siete Rayos work by heat, blood, and force, Tiembla Tierra's power is stillness and clarity — Palo's mpungo pantheon is structured around this hot/cool polarity as much as by individual domain.
figures.provenance: Lydia Cabrera, El Monte (1954); Natalia Bolívar Aróstegui, Ta Makuende Yaya (1998).

figures.gloss: Mpungo of rivers, gold, and sensual love; mistress of sweet waters, commonly equated with Ochún in Cuban syncretic practice.
figures.facet: Completes a deliberate pairing the roster otherwise leaves half-drawn: Madre de Agua holds the ocean's saltwater immensity, Mama Chola the river's intimate sweetwater currents — the same maternal-water domain split by salinity.

figures.gloss: 'Foot-in-Sore', mpungo of illness, epidemics, and the hospital of the dead, commonly equated with Babalú Ayé.
figures.facet: A major working power invoked for the specific affliction of disease rather than the general dominion of the grave, distinguishing his hospital of the sick from Centella Ndoki's cemetery gate.
figures.provenance: Natalia Bolívar Aróstegui, Ta Makuende Yaya (1998).

figures.gloss: The vast, saturating sea of the dead inherited from Kongo cosmology; in Palo, the ocean-like ambience of el muerto in general, out of which specific dead like the nfumbi condense to be bound and put to work.
figures.facet: The cosmological ground the whole religion is organized around: the nganga is not a sealed-off machine but a local intake valve on this ambient, undifferentiated sea of the dead.
figures.provenance: Todd Ramón Ochoa, Society of the Dead (2010).

figures.gloss: The 'scratching' initiation in which cuts are made in the initiate's skin before the nganga, sealing the pact of mutual obligation between palero, prenda, and nfumbi.
figures.facet: The ordeal every rostered pact presupposes: no palero has standing to command an nganga's nfumbi without first having been permanently marked by this rite.
figures.provenance: Documented across Palo ethnography, including Lydia Cabrera, El Monte (1954), and Todd Ramón Ochoa, Society of the Dead (2010).

figures.gloss: Divination by casting shell or coconut-rind discs; the pattern of faces up or down delivers the verdicts of the nfumbi and mpungos on the question asked.
figures.facet: The tradition's everyday interface with its powers: where the nganga is built and fed only occasionally, chamalongos is consulted routinely, making the dead's judgment a constant, checkable presence in daily practice.
figures.provenance: Documented across Palo ethnography, including Lydia Cabrera, El Monte (1954).

figures.gloss: The 'eyes of the dead' — a horn (mpaka) sealed with a mirror through which the palero scries the nganga's knowledge, used to diagnose illness, locate the source of a working, or see what is hidden from ordinary sight.
figures.facet: Where chamalongos yields a binary yes/no verdict, Vititi Mensu is the nganga complex's instrument of open-ended sight — the mirror-seal making the nfumbi's vision available to human eyes.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The nganga provides the physical universe and engine; the nfumbi provides the animating spirit that does the work.
Intra-tradition: The drawn signature maps the energy that the nganga generates, directing it specifically into the physical world.
Intra-tradition: Nzambi is the unreachable creator, while Lucero is the immediate threshold that must be crossed to access any divine power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct linguistic and cultural survival of the supreme Kongo creator god in the Cuban diaspora.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Highly syncretized in Cuba; Lucero acts as the Palo equivalent of the Yoruba crossroads opener.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are tricksters, psychopomps, and masters of the crossroads who alone can open the boundary between realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ogun and Zarabanda are explicitly equated in Cuban practice as the uncompromising lords of iron, war, and blood.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Lords of the forge and masters of the transformative, technological power of iron.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Deeply conflated in Cuba, both ruling lightning, fire, and a fiery, sorcerous masculine kingship.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Explosive, weapon-wielding storm deities whose natural domain is thunder and striking, immediate justice.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the vast, overwhelming, and deeply maternal matrix of the ocean and deep waters.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Fierce, absolute queens of the underworld who preside over the dead and demand strict appeasement.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both govern the grim, physical reality of the grave, the cemetery gates, and the ancestral dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Palo nganga is a direct, expanded evolution of the Kongo nkisi—a vessel that physically houses and utilizes spiritual power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are structured, physical microcosms containing all the raw materials and cosmic seeds necessary to represent the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are ultimate, fabricated engines of supernatural power that generate reality-altering force and wealth for their possessor.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Draws on Kongo ancestral logic, though Palo focuses specifically on the magical pact with physical bones rather than general bloodline ancestors.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Kongo cosmogram physically survived and evolved into the intricate patipembas (drawn magical seals) of Palo.
Intra-tradition: the cool, still mpungo of the mountain against the hot, blood-fed mpungo of iron — Palo's own hot/cool polarity organizing its pantheon.
Cross-tradition: the standard Cuban equation of the Kongo-derived mpungo of peace and purity with the Yoruba-derived orisha of the same domain, documented in Cabrera and Bolívar.
Intra-tradition: the sweetwater and saltwater halves of Palo's water-mpungo complex — river and ocean, love and immensity.
Intra-tradition: two distinct faces of Palo's death-complex — Cobayende the affliction that kills, Centella Ndoki the cemetery gate the dead pass through afterward.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: direct survival of the Kongo cosmological sea of the dead in the Cuban diaspora, the same relationship already recognized between palo-nzambi and nzambi-a-mpungu.
Intra-tradition: per Ochoa, specific nfumbi are understood to condense as particularized dead out of the ambient, undifferentiated Kalunga.
Intra-tradition: the rite that seals a palero's standing to work with a specific nganga and its nfumbi.

Cross-tradition: the two major Afro-Cuban reglas each hold one irreversible bodily initiation that inducts a devotee into lifelong ritual service — El Rayamiento's scratching and El Asiento's 'making the saint', structurally parallel but institutionally distinct.
Intra-tradition: the divination interface through which the nfumbi's and mpungos' verdicts are routinely obtained.
Cross-tradition: both cast objects to read the pattern of their fall as the verdict of spiritual powers, though Ifá's corpus of odu is a vastly more elaborated system than the discs' binary readings.
Intra-tradition: the seeing-instrument of the nganga complex, cited to the same ethnography (Cabrera, El Monte) as the cauldron itself.

Cuban cross-pollination: Oyá the Lucumí storm-and-cemetery-gate Oricha and Centella Ndoki, Palo's 'Lady of the Cemetery,' occupy the same ritual niche for practitioners who cross-initiate in both Kongo- and Yoruba-derived Cuban houses.

dossier.essence: An Afro-Cuban religion (Las Reglas de Congo, or Palo Monte) rooted in the Kongo and wider Central-African traditions of enslaved people and developed in Cuba from the colonial period onward. Its heart is the nganga (or prenda) — a consecrated iron cauldron packed with earth, sticks (palos), iron, and human bone that houses a spirit of the dead (nfumbi) with whom the initiate, a palero or tata nganga, contracts to work in the world. It is a living tradition, in Cuba and its diaspora.
dossier.cosmos: A remote creator, Nzambi (Nzambi a Mpungu), stands aloof, having left the world's daily governance to nature-powers called mpungu — Lucero at the crossroads, Zarabanda of iron, Siete Rayos of lightning, Madre de Agua of the sea, Centella of the cemetery — and, above all, to the dead. The Kongo cosmogram, a cross within a circle mapping the daily and lifelong crossing between the world of the living and the watery land of the dead (kalunga), underlies its sense of reality, and power is drawn from the pact struck with the dead inside the nganga.
dossier.practice: One enters through 'rayamiento,' an initiation of ritual scarification into a temple-house (munanso); the nganga is built, consecrated, and fed with blood, and spirits are summoned and directed by drawing 'firmas' (patipembas), chalk cosmograms specific to each mpungu and task. The work is pragmatic — healing, protection, divination, and sorcery — and lineages divide into 'baptized' (cristiana) and 'unbaptized' (judía) houses under a tata or yaya nganga.
dossier.canon: No scripture: an oral and practical tradition of ritual songs (mambos), the graphic firmas, and initiated know-how, classically documented in Lydia Cabrera's fieldwork rather than in sacred books.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the instrumental pact with the dead — religion as a working, contractual partnership with a bound spirit inside a fabricated microcosm, harnessing the power of the dead to act directly on reality.
sources: Stephan Palmié, 'Wizards and Scientists: Explorations in Afro-Cuban Modernity and Tradition' (2002)
Todd Ramón Ochoa, 'Society of the Dead: Quita Manaquita and Palo Praise in Cuba' (2010)
Bárbaro Martínez-Ruiz, 'Kongo Graphic Writing and Other Narratives of the Sign' (2013)
